symbols: kneeling posture — worship and submission before the divine
gifts or offerings — tribute brought to the Lord
Latin inscription ‘Te Adoremus Domine’ — ‘We adore you, Lord’
gilded ornamental frame — sacred and precious nature of worship
description: A carved and painted relief panel set within an ornate gilded frame with Celtic or foliate patterns. The panel depicts three figures kneeling or bending forward, each wearing robes and head coverings. They appear to hold objects or gifts. The figures are rendered in bold colors: blue, red, and dark tones. Below the scene, a Latin inscription reads ‘TE ADOREMUS DOMINE.’ The style is simplified and hieratic, evoking early medieval or folk art traditions.
theological_description: The inscription ‘Te Adoremus Domine’ (‘We adore you, Lord’) frames this as a scene of worship and adoration. The three figures bearing gifts strongly evoke the visit of the Magi who came from the East to worship the newborn King, bringing gold, frankincense, and myrrh (Matthew 2:1-11). Their kneeling posture embodies the response of the nations to the revelation of God in Christ, fulfilling the prophecy of Psalm 72:10-11 that all kings shall fall down before him. The scene captures the universal call to worship expressed in Philippians 2:10-11, that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow.

scripture_refs.reason: The three kneeling figures with gifts evoke the Magi bringing offerings to the Christ Child
All kings shall fall down before him, all nations shall serve him
At the name of Jesus every knee should bow, embodied in the adoring posture of the figures
